danger_signals.visual_form: Male humanoid enemies with aggressive postures in urban/construction environments
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate physical threat from organized criminal elements

danger_signals.visual_form: Armed enemy sprites controlling buildings and facilities
danger_signals.meaning: Coordinated, ideological threat requiring strategic intervention

danger_signals.visual_form: Non-humanoid enemy designs appearing in outer space levels
Metallic, geometric enemy sprites with mechanical behavior patterns
danger_signals.meaning: Technological threat, potentially automated or remote-controlled danger

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual variant or corrupted version of protagonist sprite
danger_signals.meaning: Personal/ideological threat; inversion of heroic values

danger_signals.visual_form: Giant crab on cruise ship; asteroid approaching Earth in arcade stages

safety_signals.visual_form: Sonic Blastman sprite with heroic stance and bright color differentiation
safety_signals.meaning: Player agency and protective intervention capability

safety_signals.visual_form: Environment transition from active danger zone to next level
safety_signals.meaning: Successful containment and progress toward mission completion

safety_signals.visual_form: Vulnerable NPCs (woman being assaulted, baby in carriage) as rescue objectives
safety_signals.meaning: Moral justification and narrative framing for player action

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Heavy Blast Man appears as corrupted/evil variant of protagonist
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Construction sites to outer space; progression from Earth-bound to cosmic threats

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Street gangs, armed groups, terrorists as distinct enemy classes
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Female enemies in JP version; replaced with male sprites in US/EU due to Nintendo censorship
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Urban construction sites, building control centers, freeway danger zones

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Mechanical vs. organic alien visuals differentiate technology-based threats
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Outer space environments; cruise ship (neutral territory invaded)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: NPCs in distress (woman assaulted, baby carriage on freeway); construction site normalcy disrupted
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Civilians flee or exhibit panic animations; threat actors pursue/control territory

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Armed gangs visible in urban spaces; buildings controlled by hostile forces; enemy density increases
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies patrol, guard positions, attack on sight; aggressive stances and weapon displays

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Progression from humanoid criminals to giant creatures to aliens to robots; environmental scale increases
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy behavior complexity increases; some enemies may use tactics or group formations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Outer space setting; giant asteroid; non-terrestrial enemy types dominate
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final boss encounter with Heavy Blast Man; ideological confrontation rather than crime prevention

environmental_storytelling.example: Game progression moves from construction site (urban, ground-level crime) → building interiors (organized crime/terrorism) → cruise ship (civilian infrastructure under attack) → outer space (existential threat). Visual environment escalation mirrors narrative threat escalation.
Early stages feature specific rescue objectives (woman being assaulted, baby in carriage on freeway). These establish hero motivation and moral legitimacy through visual vulnerability displays.

Early stages contain humanoid thugs/gangs (social order breakdown); mid-stages add terrorists/armed groups (organized threat); late stages shift to aliens/robots (system-level failure, external invasion). Enemy visual design communicates what type of crisis the world is experiencing.

Final confrontation with Heavy Blast Man (clone/dark reflection) serves as visual metaphor for ideological threat rather than resource-scarcity or territory-control threat. The enemy is not 'other' but corrupted 'self.'

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Visible but not immediately attacking; indicate territorial control and threat presence
Aggressive pursuit, coordinated attack patterns; demonstrate organized threat capability
Grab mechanics, charged attacks (D. Punch), special sonic wave attacks; show increasing power escalation through attack repertoire
Temporary dizziness/vulnerability after special attacks; balances power with tactical risk
Can be shaken, punched, thrown in directional variants, or hit with sonic blast; visual feedback of environmental manipulation shows player agency
Motionless or fleeing; require player intervention within time/threat window; establish narrative urgency

notes: Sonic Blast Man SNES is a 1992 beat-em-up unrelated to Sonic the Hedgehog franchise. The game uses visual escalation of threat types (street crime → organized terrorism → alien invasion → personal ideological conflict) as its primary world-state communication mechanism. Regional censorship (female enemy sprites in JP version replaced with male in US/EU) represents a meta-level manifestation choice imposed by publisher policy rather than game design intent. The game's narrative progression mirrors classic superhero crisis escalation: local crime → coordinated threat → external invasion → final ideological confrontation. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on location progression and enemy type variation rather than environmental decoration or NPC dialogue.
